A remote attacker can exploit a Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) header injection vulnerability in Keycloak's User-Managed Access (UMA) token endpoint. This flaw occurs because the `azp` claim from a client-supplied JSON Web Token (JWT) is used to set the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header before the JWT signature is validated. When a specially crafted JWT with an attacker-controlled `azp` value is processed, this value is reflected as the CORS origin, even if the grant is later rejected. This can lead to the exposure of low-sensitivity information from authorization server error responses, weakening origin isolation, but only when a target client is misconfigured with `webOrigins: [\"*\"]`.", "epss": {"percentage": 0.00253, "percentile": 0.16575}, "ghsa_id": "GHSA-5v8v-xvjv-57x7", "github_reviewed_at": "2026-04-08T00:08:09Z", "html_url": title: Keycloak vulnerable to information disclosure via CORS header injection due to unvalidated JWT azp claim
